coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots dominated photoelectric surface in titanium dioxide nanotube/nitrogen-doped carbon dot/gold nanocomposites for improved photoelectrochemical water splitting
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Fan
Shi, Yandi
Dang, Qian
Yang, Haiwei
Huang, Hui
Kang, Zhenhui
Shao, Mingwang
coredata.dc:description: The dynamic behavior of electron-hole pairs at the interface of the nanocomposites is important for photoelectrochemical catalysis, but it is difficult to characterize. Here we construct a ternary titanium dioxide/nitrogen-doped carbon dot/gold (TiO2/NCD/Au) complex as the model catalyst to investigate the kinetic indexes at their interfaces. Under irradiation (200 mW cm−2), the photocurrent density of TiO2/NCD/Au is 10.26 mA cm−2, which is higher than those of TiO2/Au (4.34 mA cm−2), TiO2/NCD (7.55 mA cm−2) and TiO2 (3.34 mA cm−2). The evolved oxygen of TiO2/NCD/Au reaches 125.8 μmol after 5000 s test. The energy bands of complexes are very similar to that of the unmodified TiO2 catalyst due to the low content modification of NCDs and Au. In addition, the transient photovoltage (TPV) tests with a series of control samples show differences about the carriers’ separation and transfer process, which verify that Au can increase the separation quantity of electron-hole pairs while NCDs play a more important role on the increase of the separation quantity and separation rate simultaneously. This work quantifies the function of each component in a composite catalyst and deepens the understanding of the catalyst interface design.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2
Gold
Carbon dots
Photoelectrochemistry
Oxygen evolution reaction

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Energy Conversion and Storage and Environmental Technologies

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology characterization of TiO2/NCD/Au. (a) SEM image showing the top view; (b) SEM image showing the cross-sectional view; (c) TEM image; (d-g) HRTEM image and partial enlarged details of each component in TiO2/NCD/Au.

Structural and elemental analysis of TiO2/NCD/Au. (a) XRD patterns of TiO2, TiO2/Au, TiO2/NCD and TiO2/NCD/Au; and XPS fine spectra for TiO2/NCD/Au: (b) Ti 2p, (c) C 1s, (d) O 1s, (e) N 1s, and (f) Au 4f.

Bandgap structures of TiO2-based catalysts. (a) UV/Vis diffuse reflectance spectra; (b) Tauc plots, the insert is the enlargement of the x-axis; (c) UPS spectra; and (d) Schematic diagram showing the bandgap structures.

The comparison of TPV curves for TiO2, TiO2/Au, and TiO2/NCD. (a) Schematic showing the TPV test mechanism (left) and data (right) in the TiO2-based catalysts; (b) and (c) the comparison of TPV curves for C-TiO2 and TiO2; (d) and (e) the comparison of TPV curves for TiO2 and TiO2/Au; (f) and (g) the comparison of TPV curves for TiO2 and TiO2/NCD. (b), (d) and (f) Maximum extraction rate of charge and charge extraction process; and (c), (e) and (g) attenuation process.

The comparison of TPV curves for TiO2/Au, TiO2/NCD, and TiO2/NCD/Au. (a) Maximum extraction rate of charge and charge extraction process, and (b) attenuation process for TiO2/Au and TiO2/NCD/Au; (c) Maximum extraction rate of charge and charge extraction process, and (d) attenuation process for TiO2/NCD and TiO2/NCD/Au.

Photoelectrocatalytic performance of TiO2, TiO2/Au, TiO2/NCD and TiO2/NCD/Au. (a) On–off photocurrent responses at bias of the open circuit potential; (b) Linear sweep voltammetry curves under dark (dash line) and light (solid line); (c) EIS curves; and (d) Mott-Schottky plots in dark.
The schematic diagram of TiO2/NCD/Au photoanodes for photoelectrocatalytic water splitting under light.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots dominated photoelectric surface in titanium dioxide nanotube/nitrogen-doped carbon dot/gold nanocomposites for improved photoelectrochemical water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Macao Institute of Materials Science and Engineering, Macau University of Science and Technology, Taipa 999078, Macau

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Macao Institute of Materials Science and Engineering
Macau University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Macao Institute of Materials Science and Engineering, Macau University of Science and Technology, Taipa 999078, Macau

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, PR China (Z. Kang).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: F. Liao and Y.D. Shi contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The parameters of A, tmax and τ are calculated from the transient photovoltage tests with three TiO2-based samples and show quantified information about the carriers’ separation and transfer process, which verify that N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) play a more important role than Au on the increase of the separation quantity and separation rate. The data predicted the OER performance of the catalysts as demonstrated by the electrochemical testing.

The dynamic behavior of electron-hole pairs at the interface of the nanocomposites is important for photoelectrochemical catalysis, but it is difficult to characterize. Here we construct a ternary titanium dioxide/nitrogen-doped carbon dot/gold (TiO2/NCD/Au) complex as the model catalyst to investigate the kinetic indexes at their interfaces. Under irradiation (200 mW cm−2), the photocurrent density of TiO2/NCD/Au is 10.26 mA cm−2, which is higher than those of TiO2/Au (4.34 mA cm−2), TiO2/NCD (7.55 mA cm−2) and TiO2 (3.34 mA cm−2). The evolved oxygen of TiO2/NCD/Au reaches 125.8 μmol after 5000 s test. The energy bands of complexes are very similar to that of the unmodified TiO2 catalyst due to the low content modification of NCDs and Au. In addition, the transient photovoltage (TPV) tests with a series of control samples show differences about the carriers’ separation and transfer process, which verify that Au can increase the separation quantity of electron-hole pairs while NCDs play a more important role on the increase of the separation quantity and separation rate simultaneously. This work quantifies the function of each component in a composite catalyst and deepens the understanding of the catalyst interface design.

ce:section.ce:para: High utilization of solar power and electricity to produce renewable energy is one of the effective ways for green sustainable development of energy and environmental remediation [1–6]. The invention of photoelectrochemical (PEC) devices provides the opportunity for the artificial fuel production without environmental pollution [7–11], in which the highly efficient catalysts are the guarantee of device performance. Semiconductor sensitized systems have attracted great attention. But they are facing the disadvantages of narrow bandgaps for the sunlight absorption, energy lever mismatch to redox reactions, fast electron-hole recombination, and poor interfacial charge transfer [12–14].

In order to solve these issues, strategies like composite construction and surface modification are proposed. Through rational design of components, these works successfully increase the absorption of sunlight and realize the bandgap control [15–18]. The composite interfaces are reported to be beneficial for suppressing the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers and accelerating the charge-transfer, which are verified by the measurement of bandgap or electrical characterization like electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and so on [19–21]. For example, EIS and Mott–Schottky plots were employed to demonstrate the increased electron-hole pairs and reduced recombination rate for the palladium-titanium dioxide (Pd-TiO2) composites [22]. Also, the in-situ Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy or Raman spectra give the information of the configuration and charge change of the catalysts [23]. The photoluminescence (PL) and PL decay spectra show the electron-hole separation efficiency and the longevity of charge carriers [24]. However, the detailed quantitative survey of interface dynamics for a complex is still lacking.

Water splitting is a simple and important reaction for the research of PEC process. TiO2 photoanode that electrolyzes hydrogen from water has been found by Fujishima and Honda [25]. Since then, much attention has been given to the modified TiO2, which can modulate its bandgap and increase the responses for the visible spectrum [26,27]. The extensive electron-hole recombination is also a limitation of TiO2 for the catalytic application. One-dimensional TiO2 has been confirmed to be better than other dimensional structures because it decreases the probability of electron-hole recombination [28,29]. TiO2 nanowires or nanotubes that grown on the surface of titanium (Ti) sheet are regarded as good substrates for direct application in photoelectrocatalysis [30]. Noble-metals and carbon materials are two modification materials to enhance the PEC property [31–34]. Both of them enhance the conductivity and light absorption of the catalyst system. In addition, the noble metals can act as the trap for the photo-induced electrons while increase the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs [35]. Gold (Au) is extensively investigated as the most promising co-catalyst to promote photocatalytic performance due to strong visible light absorption through the localized surface plasmon resonance [36]. Carbon material modification also improves the photosensitive properties and modulates the bandgap structure of TiO2 [37]. The interfaces between the TiO2 and these modified materials are crucial, which need to be investigated in detail.

In this work, a ternary TiO2/nitrogen-doped carbon dot/Au (TiO2/NCD/Au) nanocomposite on the Ti sheet is constructed as a model to investigate the kinetic indexes at the interfaces. Three different interfaces of TiO2/NCD, TiO2/Au and TiO2/NCD/Au are included in such a system. The transient photovoltage (TPV) curves of commercial TiO2 (C-TiO2), TiO2, TiO2/Au, TiO2/NCD and TiO2/NCD/Au are compared and give out the detailed information about carriers’ separation and transfer process, which verify that NCDs play a more important role on the increase of the separation quantity and separation rate at the same time. NCDs dominate the photoelectric surface in TiO2/NCD/Au nanocomposites. The PEC water splitting experiment confirms the above analysis. The photocurrent density of TiO2/NCD/Au is 10.26 mA cm−2, higher than those of TiO2/Au (4.34 mA cm−2) and TiO2/NCD (7.55 mA cm−2). This work quantifies the function of each component and deepens the understanding of the interfacial dynamics for a complex catalyst.

Ti foils (0.1 mm, >99.9%), hydrofluoric acid (HF, 40%), nitric acid (HNO3) and sodium sulfate (Na2SO4) are purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. C-TiO2 is obtained from the Hwrk Chemical Regent Co. Chloroauric acid tetrahydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O) is purchased from Aladdin Chemical Regent Co. All the reagents are of analytical grade without further purification. Double-distilled water is used throughout the experiment.

Pure TiO2 with nanotube structure (defined as TiO2 in the following text) and NCDs were synthesized according to the references [38,39]. The detailed processes are described in the Supporting Information.

To modify the TiO2 with NCDs, 10 mg NCDs were mixed into the electrolyte that contained 120 mg ammonium fluoride, 39 mL ethylene glycol and 1 mL deionized water. The Ti sheet was electro-oxidized as anode and graphite rod as cathode at 50 V for 40 min. Then the electro-oxidized samples were dried and transferred to muff furnace, annealing at 450 °C for 3 h to obtain TiO2/NCDs.

TiO2/Au was prepared by reducing HAuCl4·4H2O to Au nanoparticles on TiO2. The annealed TiO2, together with the Ti sheets, was immersed in a 1 mM HAuCl4 aqueous solution for 1 min. After drying, the wafers were boiled for 1 min in water/ethanol (V/V = 2:3) mixture solution at 130 °C, and Au was thermal reduced on TiO2. Such a short reaction time guarantees the low content of Au.
TiO2/NCD/Au was prepared by the same method as TiO2/Au except that the raw material was TiO2/NCD.

The TPV curves were measured by a homemade measurement system. The TiO2-based samples were excited with a pulse laser (Nd:YAG laser, Polaris II, New Wave Research, Inc.). The laser wavelength is 355 nm, and the pulse width is 5 ns. The TPV signals were registered via a digital phosphor oscilloscope (MDO3104, Tektronix).

The PEC performance measurements were carried out by instrument CHI 750 (Chen Hua, China). The classical three-electrode system was used to test the catalysts. The electrolyte is 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. Ag/AgCl was used as the reference electrode and platinum plate as the counter electrode. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves were recorded at the scan rate of 10 mV∙s−1. Mott-Schottky test was also carried out in 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. EIS curves were conducted in the frequency range of 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz with 5 mV voltage amplitude. The light source was a xenon lamp (PLS-SXE308, China) with whole wavelength and power density of 200 mW∙cm−2.

The morphological features of TiO2/NCD/Au were first characterized via scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) as shown in Fig. 1 a to 1c. The top-view (Fig. 1a) and cross-section (Fig. 1b) of the sample reveal the highly-ordered structure of TiO2/NCD/Au. The sample showed a uniform tubular structure with an average aperture of 80–90 nm and an average length of 6 μm. TEM samples were obtained by ultrasonic TiO2/NCD/Au with ethanol from the Ti sheet. The TEM image exhibited in Fig. 1c confirms the diameter of the TiO2 nanotube. Trace Au nanoparticles are loaded on the nanotube diameter. The NCDs are inserted in the nanotubes which can be seen in the high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in Fig. 1d. From the partial enlarged details in Fig. 1d, the lattice fringes are observed. The lattice fringe spacing of 0.35 nm and 0.235 nm are in accordance to the (101) crystal plane of anatase TiO2 (Fig. 1e) and (111) crystal plane of face centered cubic Au respectively (Fig. 1g) [40]. The lattice fringe spacing of 0.33 nm is corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of NCDs (Fig. 1f) [41].

In addition to morphology characterizations, the composition of TiO2/NCD/Au is demonstrated by energy dispersive spectra (EDS) in Fig. S2, which reveals the element distribution in the TiO2/NCD/Au composite. From the EDS mapping results, carbon (C), nitrogen (N) and Au elements are distributed in the same range as Ti and oxygen (O), indicating that NCDs and Au are uniformly loaded on TiO2.

Then the TiO2, TiO2/Au, TiO2/NCD and TiO2/NCD/Au samples that grow on the Ti sheets are characterized by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD). As shown in the XRD patterns in Fig. 2 a, the diffraction peaks located at 25.3, 37.8, 48.0, 53.9, 55.1, 62.7, 70.3 and 76.0° are corresponding to the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (220) and (301) crystal planes of tetragonal TiO2 (JCDPS No. 21-1272), while the peaks at 38.4, 40.2, 53.0, 62.9, 70.7, 76.2° are related to the (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (112) crystal planes of hexagonal Ti (JCDPS No. 44-1294) [42]. The characteristic peaks of Au and NCDs do not appear because their contents are lower than the minimum XRD detection limit. The Raman spectra for TiO2, TiO2/Au, TiO2/NCD and TiO2/NCD/Au are shown in Fig. S3a. The D and G peaks are observed in TiO2/NCD and TiO2/NCD/Au samples (Figs. S3d and S3f). Four peaks at 146 cm−1 (Eg), 399 cm−1 (B1g), 517 cm−1 (A1g + B1g), and 639 cm−1 (Eg) are all attributed to anatase TiO2 [43], which indicate that the modification of NCDs and Au nanoparticles do not change the basic structure of anatase TiO2.

The attachment of the NCDs to TiO2 was then confirmed by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR). As shown in Fig. S3b, the peak appeared at 3128 cm−1 is corresponding to CONH2, and the peaks between 1310 cm−1 and 1467 cm−1 are attributed to CNH and CN amide groups [44]. The peak between 500 cm−1 and 764 cm−1 is due to the vibration of TiO bonds in TiO2. The peaks belonging to NCDs are observed in TiO2/NCD samples, confirming the successful attachment of NCDs on TiO2.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra for TiO2/NCD/Au are shown in Fig. 2b-2f and Fig. S4. The TiO2/NCD/Au is collected by ultrasonic the Ti sheet supported sample and loaded on the indium support. The full spectrum indicated the existence of C, O, N, Ti and Au elements (Fig. S4), which is in accordance with the EDS results. The fine Ti 2p spectrum (Fig. 2b) shows that the Ti element in the nanocomposites is in the oxidation state of + 4. As shown in Fig. 2c, the peaks at 284.6, 286.1 and 288.4 eV are assigned to CC, CN/CO and CO bonds. For O 1s signal (Fig. 2d), the peaks at 530.2, 532.2 and 533.9 eV are attributed to TiO, OH and CO bonds, respectively. The peaks appeared at 397.4, 399.8 and 402.6 eV are corresponding to Pyridinic-N, Pyrrolic-N and Graphitic-N in the N 1s spectrum in Fig. 2e [45]. These spectra fully demonstrated the existence of NCDs in TiO2/NCD/Au sample. Fig. 2f showed the Au 4f spectra. The peak position of Au has a small shift to the low binding energy region as compared with pure Au (Fig. S5a), indicating that the plasmonic electric-field effect of inner Au nanolayer could attract electrons and promote electrons/holes separation [46]. The binding energy of Ti in TiO2 is also compared with Ti in TiO2/NCD/Au (Fig. S5b). The low content of Au and NCDs do not affect the binding energy of Ti obviously.

To be an eligible photoelectrocatalyst, the basic condition is that the bandgap of the catalysts meet the thermodynamic requirement for water splitting. The ultraviolet–visible (UV/Vis) diffuse reflectance spectra of four catalysts are shown in Fig. 3 a. TiO2, TiO2/Au, TiO2/NCD and TiO2/NCD/Au all exhibited intense light absorption in the UV region. According to
( α h ν )
1 / 2
= A
( h ν -
E g
)
, their band gaps are 3.12 eV, 3.09 eV, 3.10 eV and 3.08 eV, respectively (Fig. 3b). Furthermore, ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) results in Fig. 3c demonstrated the valence band (VB) values of semiconductors. According to the formula calculation:
E
VB
= -
[ 21.22 e V -
E
cutoff
-
E
femi
]
, the VB value of TiO2 is calculated as −7.67 eV. According the above data, the corresponding energy band diagrams of TiO2-based catalysts are shown in Fig. 3d [47]. After the modification of NCDs and Au, the bandgap of TiO2 do not change obviously. The standard electrode potential for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is 1.23 V. As shown in Fig. 3d, all the catalysts meet the bandgap requirement for OER.

As mentioned above, the four catalysts including TiO2, TiO2/Au, TiO2/NCD and TiO2/NCD/Au all satisfy the thermodynamic requirement for OER. And the bandgap values of the four catalysts are very similar due to the low content of the modification materials (NCDs and Au). In the ternary TiO2/NCD/Au catalytic system, there are many kinds of interfaces. In order to illuminate the kinetic indexes of each part in the TiO2/NCD/Au catalyst, a series of TPV measurements are performed.

The TPV test is conducted in a home-made instrument and the mechanism is shown in Fig. 4 a. A pulse laser is irradiated on the samples and results in the separation of electron-hole pairs. The different interfaces in the nanocomposites give variable separation kinetic information as shown in the right part of Fig. 4a. A is the area of the curve with green color, which means the number of electrons induced by pulse laser. The time that the photo-induced electron reaches to the maximum value is defined as tmax. Small tmax valve means fast charge extraction process. Time attenuation constants (τ) reflect the speed of the electron-hole pair recombination process. The overall diagrams of five samples are shown in Fig. S6 including C-TiO2, TiO2, TiO2/Au, TiO2/NCD and TiO2/NCD/Au. The intensity values are normalized. From these curves, the TiO2/NCD/Au catalyst performed the highest laser response characteristics.

Next, the samples are compared for detail. The intensity of the samples was all normalized according to the curve with higher intensity in one figure. The TPV curves of C-TiO2 and TiO2 were shown in Fig. 4b. The peak intensities of laser response for the two samples are very close. However, the difference of maximum extraction rate of charge and charge extraction process between these two samples can be obtained after magnification (Fig. 4b). The charge extraction process of C-TiO2 (tmax1) is faster than that of TiO2 (tmax2), indicating that C-TiO2 has the faster electron transmission capacity. The C-TiO2 is nanoparticles with diameter of 5 nm. Its SEM and TEM images are shown in Fig. S7. The uniform sphere structure is beneficial for the electron transmission to a steady state. Meanwhile, the cumulative extracted charge of TiO2 is larger, which could be obtained from the integrated areas (A2 > A1, dash areas in Fig. 4b), indicating the whole electron-hole pairs of TiO2 are larger than that of C-TiO2. The attenuation constants of C-TiO2 and TiO2 are compared as shown in Fig. 4c. The τ2 for TiO2 is larger than τ1 for C-TiO2, demonstrating that the electron-hole pair recombination process of TiO2 is slower than that of C-TiO2, which may be caused by the unique nanotube structure. It is interesting to note that a sharp peak exists at the beginning in the TPV curve of TiO2 (also exist in other TiO2 based catalysts), but is not observed in the C-TiO2. The surface of TiO2 prepared has many surface functional groups, defects and number of adsorbates [48,49], which results fast charge redistribution when the laser is irradiated.

The magnification curves of TiO2 and TiO2/Au are shown in Fig. 4d. The charge extraction process of TiO2 (tmax2) is faster than that of TiO2/Au (tmax3) but the maximum charge extraction efficiency of TiO2/Au (A3) is larger than that of TiO2 (A2), indicating that the addition of Au can increase the separation efficiency of carriers of TiO2. The values of maximum charge extraction efficiency could also agree with the saturated photocurrent density of TiO2/Au and TiO2. In Fig. 4e, the attenuation process of TiO2 is smooth (with only one attenuation constant). However, the attenuation process of TiO2/Au has several different intervals with different attenuation constants. Au acts as electron trap for the photo-induced electrons, which is the center of electron absorption in the system. Some electrons are stored in Au when the electron and holes are separated, and the stored electrons are stepwise released during the electron-hole recombination process. This is also in accordance to the result that the Au peak in the XPS spectrum (Fig. 2f) is shifted to the low binding energy direction.

In the same way, the role of NCDs in the system is analyzed. Fig. 4f displayed the enlarged TPV curves of TiO2 and TiO2/NCD. The charge extraction process of TiO2/NCD (tmax4) is faster than that of TiO2 (tmax2), which indicated that the introduction of NCDs increases the separation rate of electrons and holes. The peak intensity of excitation of TiO2/NCD is larger than TiO2 and the maximum charge extraction efficiency of TiO2/ NCD (A4) is larger than TiO2 (A2), suggesting that more electrons and holes are separated after introducing NCDs. In the attenuation curves in Fig. 4g, the time attenuation process of TiO2/NCD can be divided into two sections. The second stage is caused by the secondary discharge process of NCDs, which act as the additional charge acceptor and donor.

After the comparison of one- and two-component catalytic systems, Fig. 5 shows the TPV details for the two- and three-component catalytic systems. Fig. 5a and 5b compared the TPV curves of TiO2/Au and TiO2/NCD/Au. With the addition of NCD, the separation rate and quantity of electrons and holes of TiO2/NCD/Au (tmax5 and A5) are both larger than those of TiO2/Au (tmax3 and A3), which is coincidence with the effect of NCDs in TiO2/NCD. TPV curves of TiO2/NCD and TiO2/NCD/Au are shown in Fig. 5c and 5d. Although the charge extraction process of TiO2/NCDs/Au is a litter slower than that of TiO2/NCDs (tmax5 > tmax4), the maximum charge extraction efficiency of TiO2/NCDs/Au (A5) is larger than that of TiO2/NCDs (A4). The results also verified that Au and NCDs in such a catalytic system plays a role on the increase of separation quantity of electron-hole pairs, and NCDs further increase the separation rate. Both the addition of Au and NCDs can deduce the recombination efficiency of electron-hole pairs as can be seen from Fig. 5b and 5d. The introduction of NCDs makes the whole electron-hole recombination process smooth without seeing the vibration process in the attenuation curves like that in TiO2/Au. Data in Fig. 5 further demonstrate that the ternary TiO2/NCD/Au composite is superior to the binary composites.

In order to verify the above TPV results, the photo-response property of materials and the ability of photoelectrocatalytic OER tests were performed in 0.5 M Na2SO4. Fig. 6 a showed the on-off photocurrent responses of four catalysts at the bias of the open circuit potential. A full wavelength light (200 mW∙cm−2) was applied every 20 s. TiO2/NCD/Au showed the maximum optical response current density of 10.26 mA∙cm−2 compared with other three catalysts. Apart from this, photocurrent densities versus voltage curves (Fig. 6b) also exhibited the photoelectric response ability of catalysts. Compared with curves under dark (dash line), the current density increased obviously under light (solid line). The onset potential was much lower than the standard potential for OER because the introduced light energy. The photo-saturation current densities of TiO2, TiO2/Au, TiO2/NCD and TiO2/NCD/Au reached 3.34, 4.34, 7.55 and 10.26 mA∙cm−2 respectively.

To further explore the reason of superior material performance, the EIS and Mott–Schottky tests were performed. The EIS results (Fig. 6c) could evaluate the surface reaction kinetics. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) is expressed by the arc radius in the Nyquist plots and smaller radius represents smaller Rct. From the results, the modification of Au and NCDs for TiO2 can both reduce Rct, among which the addition of Au shows more improvement effect on electrical conductivity. What’s more, the ternary TiO2/NCD/Au composite showed minimal Rct, indicating the synergistic effect of three components. To study the charge carrier density (ND) information of the semiconductors, Mott-Schottky plots were recorded in dark. As shown in Fig. 6d, the four catalysts were all n-type semiconductors due to the positive slopes. The ND is calculated by the following equation [50]:
N D
=
2
e ε
ε 0
dE
d ( 1 /
C
2
)
where e is the electron charge, ε is the dielectric constant of anatase TiO2 (ε = 48), ε0 (8.86 × 10−12 F/m) is the vacuum permittivity, C is the space charge capacitance, and E is the potential. The calculated values of ND for TiO2, TiO2/Au, TiO2/NCD, TiO2/NCD/Au were 2.90 × 1020, 3.52 × 1020, 3.41 × 1020, 6.26 × 1020 cm−3 respectively. Upon the addition of Au or NCD, the values increase slightly and 2.2-fold enhancement in the value is noticed for TiO2/NCD/Au. The PL spectra of the four samples were obtained under the excitation wavelength of 310 nm (Fig. S8). The PL intensity of TiO2/NCD/Au decreased significantly compared with TiO2, TiO2/NCD and TiO2/Au, indicating that it can restrain the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes under photoexcitation effectively [24,51]. After the addition of Au and NCDs, the conductivity of the catalysts greatly increases, which is in accordance with the EIS results, further demonstrating the synergistic effect of three components. The stability of the four photoanodes is performed by i-t measurement (Fig. S9a). The photocurrent densities of TiO2/NCD/Au remained relatively stable throughout the process, maintaining 90% of the initial currents after 5000 s. The ratio of evolved O2 and H2 by TiO2/NCD/Au that tested by gas chromatography (Fig. S9b) is close to the theoretical value.

From the above discussion, the roles of each component are clear, the schematic of which is shown in Fig. 7 . When the light source is illuminated, the semiconductor TiO2 is excited and the electron-holes are separated. Fast electron-hole recombination rate limits the photoelectrocatalytic water splitting efficiency. When Au is attached on the surface of TiO2, it acts as an electron absorption center, leaving holes that facilitate water oxidation. However, it may slow the charge extraction process (tmax3 > tmax2). In addition, Au cannot store electrons. The electrons aggregate on its surface and release stepwise due to the surface polarization, which is demonstrated by the attenuation curves in Fig. 4e. Compared with Au, NCDs perform dual functions in this system. It serves as electron traps for the photoinduced electrons from the conduction band (CB) of TiO2 under UV light, which is similar as Au. It also acts as a photosensitizer under visible light, donating the electrons to the CB of TiO2. Accompany by the roles of electron donor and acceptor to promote the efficiency of electron-hole separation, the number of electrons and holes produced by photo-excitation can also be greatly improved as verified by the increased area of the extracted charge (A4 > A2 in Fig. 4f). The number of holes involved in the OER reaction increases. The charge extraction rate of TiO2/NCD is smaller than those of TiO2 and TiO2/Au (tmax4 < tmax2 < tmax3). With NCDs in the nanocomposites, the attenuation curve is smooth with two time attenuation constants (Fig. 4g). NCDs facilitate the electron-hole separation rate and lead to a gentler electron release process, which show its strong electron transfer capacity. When TiO2, Au and NCDs are combined together, the accumulative action makes the TiO2/NCD/Au exhibit the best PEC water splitting activity. More extracted electrons are from NCD as A5/A3 > A5/A4 and the process with NCD is faster as tmax4 < tmax5 < tmax3 (Fig. 5a and 5c). The attenuation process of TiO2/NCD/Au is similar to TiO2/NCD with two time attenuation constants (τ5 and τ5′). So, in such a ternary system, NCDs dominate the photoelectric surface.

A ternary composite system of TiO2/NCD/Au photoanode catalyst was constructed with different electron transport interface. The photocurrent densities of TiO2/NCD/Au, TiO2/NCD and TiO2/Au are 10.26 mA cm−2, 7.55 mA cm−2 and 4.34 mA cm−2, respectively. Compared with previous researches on the interface of the catalysts based on steady-state test methods [22,27,34], here the interfacial dynamics between TiO2/Au, TiO2/NCD and TiO2/NCD/Au were intensively investigated with the TPV test from the calculated parameters of A, tmax and τ. Although NCDs and Au showed synergistic effect to enhance the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs of TiO2 (also confirmed by former works [43]), their contributions were different as verified by the detailed TPV data. Compared with Au, NCDs play a more important role on the increase of the separation quantity and separation rate in the TiO2/NCD/Au nanocomposites. This work provides a convenient and efficient TPV method for the dynamics analysis of the electron generation and transport in a light response system. The quantified parameter values give theoretical basis for predicting the catalyst performances, which can be applied in the screening of catalysts rapidly.

Fan Liao: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yandi Shi: Visualization, Data curation. Qian Dang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Haiwei Yang: Resources. Hui Huang: Project administration. Zhenhui Kang: Writing – review & editing. Mingwang Shao: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National MCF Energy R&D Program (2018YFE0306105), National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFA0204800, 2020YFA0406103, 2020YFA0406104, 2020YFA0406101), Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51821002), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51902217, 91961120, 51725204, 21771132, 51972216, 52041202‬), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190041), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the 111 Project and Joint International Research Laboratory of Carbon-Based Functional Materials and Devices.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.08.131.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
